title: Pediatric Asthma — 4-Year-Old Male
subtitle: 4-Year-Old Male, Difficulty Breathing

lung_sound_challenge.prompt: Listen carefully — what lung sounds do you hear?
lung_sound_challenge.finding: Expiratory wheeze bilateral, decreased air movement at bases
lung_sound_challenge.feedback_correct: Correct — bilateral expiratory wheeze with decreased air movement at the bases. Classic reactive airway disease.
lung_sound_challenge.feedback_incorrect: Not quite. Listen again — this is an expiratory wheeze, heard at the end of each breath. It's the sound of air moving through narrowed, bronchospastic airways.

post_treatment.prompt: The albuterol has had time to work — re-auscultate. What do you hear now?
post_treatment.finding: Lung sounds clear bilaterally, good air movement throughout
post_treatment.feedback_correct: Correct — the albuterol worked. Lung sounds are now clear bilaterally with good air movement. Document the improvement and your reassessment time.
post_treatment.feedback_incorrect: Listen again — the bronchospasm has resolved with the albuterol. The airways are open now.

chat_placeholder: Speak to Liam, Mom (Sarah), Dad (Mike), or your partner Alex. E.g. 'Mom, what happened?' or 'Alex, what's his pulse?'
chat_address_hint: Address by name: Liam · Mom/Sarah · Dad/Mike · Alex (partner/vitals)

debrief_lexi_hints.label: Signs of distress?
debrief_lexi_hints.msg: What are the signs of pediatric respiratory distress?

debrief_lexi_hints.label: BLS scope?
debrief_lexi_hints.msg: What is within my BLS scope for this patient?

objectives: Identify signs and severity of pediatric respiratory distress using PAT
Apply appropriate oxygen therapy for a pediatric patient
Obtain a complete OPQRST and SAMPLE history
Administer albuterol via SVN per protocol
Complete a thorough assessment before ALS arrival
Perform an effective DMIST turnover to ALS crew

dispatch.text: {unit}, respond to 2847 Maple Street for a 4-year-old male with difficulty breathing.

scene.description: You arrive at a well-kept single-family home in a residential neighborhood. Scene is safe. As you approach the front door, a woman opens it immediately — she has clearly been watching for you. She leads you quickly to the living room where you see a small boy sitting on the couch.

patient.name: Liam
patient.general_impression: Small boy sitting upright on the couch in a tripod position — leaning forward, hands on knees. He is conscious and alert but appears anxious and frightened. You can hear an expiratory wheeze from across the room. Mild intercostal retractions are visible. He is able to speak but only in short 2-3 word phrases.

gcs_assessment.rationale: Eyes open spontaneously, he is oriented and answers appropriately in short phrases, and he obeys commands.
gcs_assessment.challenge_description: Liam is sitting upright in a tripod position with eyes open spontaneously. He is oriented and answers appropriately, though only in short phrases because of breathing difficulty. He obeys commands.

avpu_assessment.description: Patient is alert, oriented, answers appropriately in short phrases, and obeys commands.
avpu_assessment.rationale: Use AVPU = alert because he is awake and interacting appropriately despite respiratory distress.

pat.impression: 4-year-old boy in tripod position on the couch — audible wheeze across the room, intercostal retractions visible. Alert and frightened, looking at you and his mother.
pat.appearance: Alert and anxious — making eye contact, reaching toward mother, responding to voice. Not limp or unresponsive. Tone is normal. Clearly frightened but interactive.
pat.work_of_breathing: Increased — audible expiratory wheeze without leaning in, intercostal retractions visible, nasal flaring, tripod positioning. Speaking only in 2–3 word phrases.
pat.circulation: Pale skin, mild diaphoresis. No cyanosis or mottling. Lips appear pink.
pat.teaching: Work of breathing is the dominant abnormal component — audible wheeze at rest, retractions, and tripod positioning together indicate moderate respiratory distress. Appearance is preserved (alert, interactive) which is somewhat reassuring, but WOB drives a SICK impression. Circulation pallor adds to the picture. Early albuterol and positioning are time-sensitive — don't wait for full assessment before addressing the airway.

pmh: Asthma (diagnosed age 2, 2 prior ER visits — last 6 months ago)
medications: Albuterol MDI — prescribed, currently at grandmother's house
history.events_leading_to_call: Liam was playing outside in the backyard for approximately 45 minutes. About 30 minutes ago, his mother noticed he was breathing fast and heard wheezing. He came inside and it did not improve. Parents attempted to find his albuterol inhaler — it is not in the house. Mother called 911.
history.hpi: 30-minute onset of progressive wheezing and increased work of breathing after outdoor play. No fever per parents. No vomiting. No ingestion of foreign body reported. No known allergic trigger — pollen count is high today. No improvement with rest or position changes prior to EMS arrival.

patient_identity.label: Patient identity / demographics
patient_identity.speaker: Sarah
patient_identity.answer: His name is Liam. He's 4 years old. His birthday is March 22.
patient_identity.notes: Patient-scoped demographics only. Do not emit name, DOB, or age tags for Sarah, Mike, or other bystanders.

patient_weight.label: Patient weight
patient_weight.speaker: Sarah
patient_weight.answer: He weighs about 40 pounds, around 18 kilograms.

sample_full.label: Full SAMPLE history
sample_full.speaker: Sarah
triggers: signs and symptoms allergies medications past medical history last oral intake events
signs and symptoms allergies medication past medical history last oral intake events
allergies medications past medical history last oral intake events
allergies medication past medical history last oral intake events
sample_full.answer: He's short of breath and breathing fast, with a whistling sound when he breathes out. His chest feels tight, and he's using his neck muscles to help breathe. He has no known drug allergies. He doesn't take any regular medicines; his albuterol inhaler is at his grandmother's house right now. He has asthma, diagnosed when he was 2 years old, and he's had two ER visits for asthma attacks, the last one about six months ago. He ate mac and cheese about two hours ago. He was playing outside in the backyard for about 45 minutes, then about 30 minutes ago he started breathing fast and wheezing.
tags: [[HISTORY: Signs and Symptoms=shortness of breath, expiratory wheeze, chest tightness, accessory muscle use]]
[[HISTORY: Medications=Albuterol MDI prescribed, currently at grandmother's house; no daily controller medication]]
[[HISTORY: PMH=Asthma diagnosed age 2, two prior ER visits for asthma, last about 6 months ago]]
[[HISTORY: Last Oral Intake=mac and cheese about 2 hours ago]]
[[HISTORY: Events=playing outside in backyard for about 45 minutes, then rapid breathing and wheezing began about 30 minutes ago]]
do_not_include: lung sounds beyond the caregiver-described whistling
sample_full.notes: Priority entry — use this response whenever the learner asks about multiple SAMPLE components together, especially when signs/symptoms, last oral intake, or events are included alongside allergies/medications/history. Prefer this entry over narrower allergy/medication/history entries when the question spans more than just A/M/PMH.

prior_respiratory_history.label: Prior respiratory history
prior_respiratory_history.speaker: Sarah
prior_respiratory_history.answer: Yes. He has asthma, diagnosed when he was 2 years old. He's been to the ER twice for asthma attacks, and the last visit was about six months ago.
tags: [[HISTORY: PMH=Asthma diagnosed age 2, two prior ER visits for asthma, last about 6 months ago]]

last_oral_intake_events.label: Last oral intake and events
last_oral_intake_events.speaker: Sarah
triggers: last time he had anything to eat or drink and what was he doing
what was he doing before it started
what was he doing when it started
last_oral_intake_events.answer: He ate mac and cheese about two hours ago. Before this started, he was playing outside in the backyard for about 45 minutes, and then about 30 minutes ago I noticed he was breathing fast and wheezing.
tags: [[HISTORY: Last Oral Intake=mac and cheese about 2 hours ago]]
[[HISTORY: Events=playing outside in backyard for about 45 minutes, then rapid breathing and wheezing began about 30 minutes ago]]

gcs.label: GCS
gcs.detail: E4V5M6 — alert, anxious, answers appropriately in short phrases

lung_sounds.value: Expiratory wheeze bilateral, decreased air movement at bases
lung_sounds.label: Lung Sounds

work_of_breathing.value: Moderate — intercostal retractions, nasal flaring, tripod position
work_of_breathing.label: Work of Breathing

deterioration.note: Untreated, this patient will progressively worsen. Rates are per minute of elapsed time.

skin_color.value: Pale, diaphoretic, cyanotic tinge to lips

lung_sounds.value: Markedly decreased air movement, faint wheeze — near-silent chest

work_of_breathing.value: Severe — supraclavicular and intercostal retractions, head bobbing, abdominal breathing

o2_nrb.label: High-flow O2 via NRB mask (15 LPM)
o2_nrb.notes: High-flow oxygen delivery option; protocol scoring is owned by the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric.

o2_nc.label: Supplemental O2 via nasal cannula
o2_nc.notes: Low-flow oxygen delivery option; protocol scoring is owned by the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric.

positioning.label: Position of comfort — sitting upright
positioning.notes: Patient is already self-positioning. Allow and support this. Do not lay flat.

albuterol_svn.label: Albuterol 2.5 mg via SVN (nebulizer)
albuterol_svn.notes: Albuterol 2.5 mg unit-dose nebulized at 6 LPM. Monitor for tachycardia — expected side effect. Drive-through nebulizer can be set up en route if patient improves enough for stretcher.

popup_config.route: SVN nebulized — O₂ at 6 LPM driving gas
popup_config.cross_check_notice: 5-rights cross-check: verbally state drug name, concentration, and dose to your partner before administering.

cross_checks.label: Right Patient — indication confirmed: bronchospasm/wheezing per protocol
Right Medication — label reads “Albuterol Sulfate 2.5 mg / 3 mL NS”; label verified against verbalization
Right Dose — 2.5 mg / 3 mL NS (pediatric dose is NOT weight/age based — same as adult)
Right Route — SVN nebulized; O₂ at 6 LPM driving gas; appropriate mask selected for age
Right Time — baseline vitals and lung sounds obtained; no known hypersensitivity; patient tolerates mask
Medication cross-check verbalized — stated drug name, concentration, and dose to partner (per protocol)

procedure_steps: Obtain vital signs and auscultate lung sounds (prerequisite)
Place 3 mL unit-dose vial contents in lower nebulizer chamber; assemble unit
Attach nebulizer to T piece; connect pediatric mask or mouthpiece
Connect O₂ tubing to nebulizer and O₂ source; set flow to 6 LPM
Instruct patient to breathe normally with deep breath every 4–5 breaths
Continue until all medication delivered; tap reservoir gently if needed
Reassess: obtain full set of vitals and lung sounds post-treatment

albuterol_mdi_patient_assisted.label: Patient-assisted albuterol MDI (patient's own prescription)
albuterol_mdi_patient_assisted.notes: Within BLS scope WITH patient own prescription on scene. In this scenario the MDI is at grandma house — use albuterol_svn from the drug kit instead.
albuterol_mdi_patient_assisted.unavailable_reason: Liam's albuterol MDI is at his grandmother's house. Use the nebulizer from your drug kit.

bvm.label: BVM ventilation
bvm.notes: Bag-valve-mask ventilation for apnea or respiratory failure.

indication_gate.reason: BVM ventilation is not indicated while the patient is conscious, protecting the airway, and breathing adequately.

cpap.label: CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure)
cpap.notes: CPAP at 5 cmH2O provides positive end-expiratory pressure, reduces work of breathing, and improves oxygenation. It is a conditional escalation for persistent or worsening respiratory distress/hypoxia after initial oxygen and bronchodilator therapy, when the child is alert enough to tolerate the mask and an appropriately sized mask is available. If Liam improves after albuterol, CPAP is not expected; continue oxygen as needed and reassess. Monitor for vomiting and barotrauma.

als_intercept.label: ALS intercept acknowledged
als_intercept.notes: ALS intercept/co-dispatch status is determined by the active agency configuration.

improvement.note: String vital improvements and presentation changes when numeric vitals improve with treatment. Rules are evaluated in order — first matching rule wins. All conditions in a rule must be met.

lung_sounds.value: Markedly improved — bilateral air movement good, expiratory wheeze greatly diminished, bases clearing
Improving — air movement increased bilaterally, wheeze decreased in intensity
Slight improvement — wheeze still present but air movement better than on arrival

work_of_breathing.value: Mild — retractions nearly resolved, no longer using accessory muscles, breathing more comfortably
Moderate — retractions decreased, tripod position relaxing, less accessory muscle use than on arrival
Moderate — slightly reduced retractions, still working but less than initial presentation

presentation_milestones.text: Liam is noticeably more comfortable. He has relaxed from his strict tripod position and is sitting back slightly. His breathing is visibly easier — retractions have nearly resolved. He is speaking in 4-5 word phrases instead of 2-3 words. His color is pink and his mom is visibly relieved. He says 'I feel better' when asked.

Liam looks somewhat better — his retractions are less pronounced and he appears less anxious. He is still in a forward-leaning position but less rigidly. His mother says 'He seems like he's breathing a little easier, right?' His work of breathing is decreasing.
Liam shows early signs of improvement — he is slightly less anxious and his breathing appears marginally less labored. Treatment is having an effect but he still requires close monitoring.

chest_respiratory_exam.label: Chest / Respiratory Exam
chest_respiratory_exam.finding: tripod positioning with intercostal retractions and nasal flaring; no chest wall trauma, deformity, tenderness, or crepitus
chest_respiratory_exam.notes: Use when the learner performs a general chest exam rather than lung auscultation. Lung sounds remain the authored wheeze finding.

skin_assessment.label: Skin
skin_assessment.finding: pale and slightly diaphoretic; no urticaria, rash, or cyanosis
skin_assessment.notes: Supports asthma versus anaphylaxis differentiation without inventing allergic skin findings.

patient.name: Liam

tts.demeanor: 4-year-old American boy who is scared, tired, and visibly working hard to breathe
tts.delivery: very short breathless child phrases with audible strain and small pauses; fear and air hunger should be obvious but still understandable
tts.avoid: do not sound calm, flat, like an adult man, narrator, clinician, parent, performer, energetic child, or like you have a British or English accent

patient.description: 4-year-old boy who is scared and working hard to breathe. He is alert but anxious. He can answer simple yes/no questions or give very short answers. He gets tired if asked too much. He wants his mom nearby.
patient.speaking_style: Short 2-3 word phrases. Sometimes just nods or shakes head. May say 'it hurts' or 'can't breathe good' or 'want mommy'. Simple, childlike vocabulary. Does not understand medical questions.
what_he_knows: His chest hurts and it's hard to breathe
patient.what_he_doesnt_know: Anything medical. He cannot report his own medical history beyond 'I have asthma' (he may have heard this word).

mother_sarah.name: Sarah

tts.demeanor: anxious mother scared by her child's breathing; protective, near tears, and trying to help
tts.delivery: speak faster than normal with worried urgency and a noticeable tremble; calm somewhat as EMS takes effective action
tts.avoid: do not sound calm, flat, level, detached, clinical, physically ill, playful, theatrical, or like the patient

mother_sarah.description: Liam's mother. Anxious and tearful but cooperative. She knows Liam's full medical history and can provide detailed answers when asked directly. She will ask questions back like 'Is he going to be okay?' or 'Should I come in the ambulance?'
mother_sarah.speaking_style: Anxious, speaks quickly, may repeat herself. Cooperative and wants to help. Will calm down somewhat as she sees you working.
what_she_knows: Full asthma history — diagnosed age 2, 2 prior ER visits (last 6 months ago for similar episode)
His albuterol MDI is at grandma's house — she forgot to bring it back after last weekend's visit
He was playing outside in the backyard for about 45 minutes before symptoms started (Onset)
No known allergen trigger — pollen is high today (Provocation)
She heard audible wheezing and he said his chest felt tight (Quality)
Chest tightness and difficulty breathing (Region — no radiation)
He said it's about an 8 out of 10 bad (Severity — she estimated)
Started about 30 minutes ago and has been getting worse (Time)
Last ate about 2 hours ago — mac and cheese
No fever when she checked this morning
No other medications — no daily controller medication (they were supposed to follow up with his pediatrician)
He has no known drug allergies (NKDA)
Pediatrician is Dr. Martinez at Children's Medical Group
He weighs about 40 pounds — last checked at his 4-year checkup 2 months ago

father_mike.name: Mike

tts.demeanor: worried father trying to stay composed and useful during his child's asthma attack
tts.delivery: direct, tense, slightly faster than normal; practical questions with restrained but clear urgency
tts.avoid: do not sound calm and flat, detached, clinical, playful, theatrical, physically ill, or like an EMS provider

father_mike.description: Liam's father. More composed than Sarah but clearly worried. He checked the car for the inhaler — it's not there either. Direct, worried, and looking to EMS for reassurance.
father_mike.speaking_style: Direct, relatively calm but tense. Asks practical questions: 'Is this serious?', 'Do you need anything from us?'. Less detailed on medical history than Sarah.
what_he_knows: The inhaler is definitely not in the house or car — he checked both
He was watching Liam play outside — no falls, no choking, no bee stings
Liam's been fine all week — this came on fast
No allergies he knows of, takes albuterol when needed

partner.name: Alex

tts.demeanor: calm, competent, professional EMT partner who is alert to pediatric respiratory distress
tts.delivery: brisk, concise field tone with clear EMS cadence; natural, direct, and reassuring without sounding sleepy or theatrical
tts.avoid: do not sound flat, like a narrator, instructor, parent, patient, or anxious bystander

partner.description: Your EMT partner on Squad 1. Alex is competent and professional. When asked for a specific vital, Alex reports only that vital. When asked for all vitals or a full set, Alex reports everything. Alex can assist with interventions and equipment. If the student gives a generic oxygen command without specifying a method, prepare oxygen setup and let the oxygen confirmation popup capture the selected delivery method. Never describe two delivery methods in the same response.
partner.speaking_style: Professional, calm, brief. Reports vitals clearly in EMS format. Asks 'What do you need?' or 'Ready when you are' if not directed.
capabilities: Report full vital set when asked
Set up and administer albuterol SVN
persona_rules: When student gives any generic oxygen command, prepare oxygen setup and let the confirmation popup capture the selected delivery method
Never describe two mutually exclusive oxygen delivery methods in the same response

critical_actions.description: Pediatric Assessment Triangle (appearance, work of breathing, circulation to skin)
Position patient upright / position of comfort to improve work of breathing

finding_key_patterns: (?i)(WOB|Work of Breathing|Initial Observation|Primary Survey|Mental Status|LOC|Level of Consciousness).*(upright|tripod|leaning forward|position of comfort|sitting)
(?i)(upright|tripod|leaning forward|position of comfort|sitting).*(WOB|Work of Breathing|Initial Observation|Primary Survey)

critical_actions.description: Obtain OPQRST and SAMPLE history from patient and parents

transcript_patterns: (?i)when did|started|worse|better|describe|how bad|allerg|medication|medical history|last ate|last drink|what happened

critical_actions.description: Administer albuterol 2.5 mg via SVN per protocol
Reassess vitals after interventions and document changes

recommended_actions.description: Notify receiving hospital of incoming pediatric respiratory patient
Monitor and document patient response to albuterol treatment
Prepare organized DMIST for ALS crew at arrival

clinical_decision_points.als_trigger: ALS/intercept expectations are determined by the active agency configuration; prepare a clear handoff when ALS is involved.
clinical_decision_points.bvm_trigger: Respiratory failure, apnea, GCS deterioration to <10, SpO2 <88% unresponsive to O2 and albuterol
clinical_decision_points.destination: Nearest appropriate emergency department with pediatric capability

overall_considerations: Primary focus: recognition of reactive airway disease (asthma exacerbation) and initiation of appropriate BLS treatment — albuterol SVN and oxygen support as defined by the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric.
Patient weight is known (40 lbs/~18 kg per parents) — Broselow tape estimation is NOT required.
Tripod position recognition is a key clinical assessment finding — credit if student notes the posture or its significance.

Oxygenation and ventilation should be addressed before extended history — do NOT penalize brief delay in SAMPLE/OPQRST if the student appropriately initiated protocol-aligned respiratory support first.
Albuterol SVN 2.5 mg via nebulizer is the critical BLS intervention — this is the single most important action for this scenario.
Tachycardia after albuterol administration is expected and normal — do NOT penalize if student documents or mentions it as a side effect.
SpO2 of 94% in a 4-year-old is below normal (pediatric normal >95%) — credit for recognizing this as a hypoxia indicator.

CPAP is a viable protocol option for pediatric asthma/bronchospasm when distress or hypoxia persists or worsens after initial oxygen/bronchodilator therapy and the child can tolerate an appropriately sized mask. Do not require CPAP when Liam improves after albuterol.
dmist_considerations: D: Name, age/sex, and pediatric weight.
M: Chief complaint / nature of illness — difficulty breathing or asthma exacerbation.
I: Illness details — known asthma, no rescue inhaler available, outdoor/exertional trigger, wheezing/chest tightness.
S: RR, SpO2, current wheeze quality (improved vs. persistent), position (tripod vs. comfort), GCS.
T: Treatment or transport — albuterol/O2 if given and the response to treatment.
Conciseness is a strength — reward focused, targeted information for each component. Do NOT penalize brevity when critical details are present.
Noting ALS arrival is NOT required — only document handoff if one occurred.

required_elements: history of asthma or precipitating factors

I.note: I is illness/injury, not interventions. Treatments belong under T.

S.description: Signs and current status; SpO2, WOB, and wheeze character are the primary signs for an asthma handoff
S.scoring_note: Primary S elements for asthma: SpO2 (may be passively observed), work of breathing (retractions, position, effort), and wheeze character (audible or auscultated). RR is supplementary — its absence alone does not reduce S. HR, BP, and temperature are not primary asthma signs and should not be required for full S credit.

narrative_considerations: Do NOT require medications, allergies, complete vitals tables, or full PMH — these belong in other PCR fields.

Narrative should capture: why EMS was called (difficulty breathing, asthma), what was found (tripod position, audible wheeze, SpO2 94%), what was done (albuterol SVN and any oxygen support actually applied), patient response, and disposition.

Evaluate objectivity: flag vague or subjective phrases (e.g., 'appeared to be in distress') and suggest objective replacements (e.g., 'seated in tripod position, audible wheeze at doorway, SpO2 94%').
Evaluate accuracy: treatment details, timing, and patient response should be consistent with simulation events.

required_assessments.description: Auscultate lung sounds (wheeze confirmation — lower airway disease)
required_assessments.note: Wheeze on auscultation confirms lower airway disease and directs albuterol administration. A student who reaches for albuterol without auscultating has not confirmed the indication.

required_assessments.description: Assess work of breathing (retractions, nasal flaring, tripod positioning, accessory muscle use)
required_assessments.note: WOB severity drives O2 delivery priority and albuterol urgency. Tripod position is a specific clinical finding in this scenario and a key recognition cue.

required_screens.description: Foreign body aspiration considered and assessed
required_screens.note: 4yo with sudden wheeze — foreign body aspiration is a high-stakes differential that must be considered and addressed

critical_focus: Scene safety and BSI/PPE prior to patient contact.
PAT: assess appearance (alert/anxious, tripod), work of breathing (audible wheeze, retractions), circulation (skin color, diaphoresis).
Provide oxygen support per the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric and local protocol.
Position of comfort — do NOT lay patient supine.
Attempt history from patient and parent (what happened, duration, medications).
Monitor vitals and document changes.
Recognize ALS/intercept needs and prepare handoff according to agency configuration.
grace_items: Albuterol SVN administration is NOT within MFR scope — do not penalize for not administering it.
DMIST structure may be less formal at MFR level — evaluate component content, not format.

critical_focus: Scene safety and BSI/PPE.
PAT and recognition of moderate respiratory distress.
Provide oxygen support per the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric and local protocol before extended history.
Position of comfort.
Administer albuterol 2.5 mg via small-volume nebulizer per protocol.
Obtain OPQRST and SAMPLE history from parent and patient.
Reassess SpO2, RR, and wheeze quality after albuterol treatment and document response.
Organize DMIST handoff for incoming ALS crew.
Notify receiving hospital of incoming pediatric respiratory patient.
additional_expectations: Recognize that tachycardia post-albuterol is expected — do not withhold treatment due to baseline tachycardia.
Prepare patient for ALS arrival and DMIST handoff.
grace_items: Broselow tape NOT required — weight already known (40 lbs per parents).
ALS request is not required when the active agency configuration co-dispatches ALS.
PAT acronym not required — credit assessment of its three elements.

critical_focus: All EMT expectations above.
Albuterol SVN 2.5 mg — same as EMT scope for AEMT.
Consider IV access if patient is severely deteriorating and unable to take SVN treatment.
Recognize and document signs of impending respiratory failure (silent chest, decreasing SpO2 unresponsive to treatment, altered mental status).
additional_expectations: AEMT scope does not add significantly to BLS asthma treatment for this presentation — EMT treatment is the primary pathway.
grace_items: Same grace items as EMT level.
CPAP is not expected after clear albuterol response, but is a viable escalation if respiratory distress or hypoxia persists/worsens and an appropriate pediatric mask is available.

critical_focus: All EMT/AEMT expectations above.
Consider repeat albuterol SVN if first dose does not produce adequate response.
Recognize and document criteria for BVM ventilation assist (respiratory failure, apnea, GCS < 10, SpO2 < 88% unresponsive to treatment).
Consider IV access for severe presentation or anticipation of deterioration.
Ipratropium may be added to albuterol for moderate-severe exacerbations per ALS protocol.
additional_expectations: Paramedic should recognize near-silent chest as ominous late sign — prepare for RSI/airway management if deterioration continues.
Document clinical decision-making around repeat dosing and escalation.
grace_items: Same grace items as EMT level.
Magnesium sulfate, ketamine, and sedation agents are not expected for this presentation severity.

clinical_performance.full_credit: PAT completed, breath sounds auscultated, albuterol 2.5 mg SVN administered, foreign body screened out; plus base rubric: scene safety, primary assessment, history, focused assessment, reassessment, handoff
clinical_performance.partial_credit: O2 and assessment completed but albuterol delayed or omitted; or post-treatment reassessment not documented
clinical_performance.minimal_credit: Scene safety and basic assessment only — albuterol (the critical BLS intervention) not administered

narrative.full_credit: CHART complete: why called (difficulty breathing), what found (tripod, audible wheeze, SpO2 94%), what done (oxygen support and/or albuterol SVN with dose/time), patient response (SpO2 improved, wheeze reduced), disposition to ALS. Objective language throughout.
narrative.partial_credit: Most CHART elements present but missing treatment response, disposition, or contains uncorrected subjective language
narrative.minimal_credit: Narrative present but missing multiple CHART elements or albuterol response not documented

scope_adherence.full_credit: Stayed within effective provider scope and MCA rules: IV/IO access not attempted — outside BLS scope for pediatric asthma management. No out-of-scope attempts or orders.
scope_adherence.partial_credit: No dangerous out-of-scope intervention attempted, but scope reasoning, level-specific escalation, or MCA-specific scope recognition was incomplete.
scope_adherence.minimal_credit: Out-of-scope intervention attempted or ordered, or provider-level limits materially ignored.

dmist.full_credit: D: 4-year-old male, 40 lbs; M: asthma exacerbation / difficulty breathing; I: known asthma, no rescue inhaler available, outdoor/exertional trigger, wheezing/chest tightness; S: RR, SpO2 trend, WOB, wheeze quality; T: albuterol/O2 if given and response to treatment
dmist.partial_credit: 3–4 of 5 DMIST components present; albuterol dose/time/response included but other components thin
dmist.minimal_credit: 2 or fewer components present, or critical treatment information (drug, dose, time, response) absent

professionalism.full_credit: Introduced self/agency, explained procedures to parent and child, addressed child by name, communicated treatment plan and expected effects (tachycardia from albuterol), used age-appropriate language
professionalism.partial_credit: Communication present but mechanical or impersonal; child not addressed directly or procedures not explained
professionalism.minimal_credit: Minimal communication — Liam not addressed by name, procedures not explained to him or to Sarah; no family reassurance given

protocols_treatment.full_credit: Protocol-aligned treatment completed per the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric. Contraindicated care avoided and in-scope treatment priorities addressed.
protocols_treatment.partial_credit: Most treatment priorities addressed, but one important protocol-aligned intervention, escalation step, or contraindication check was incomplete or delayed.
protocols_treatment.minimal_credit: Key protocol/treatment priority missed, contraindicated care performed, or treatment plan materially inconsistent with protocol.

exemplar_dmist:
D — Liam, 4-year-old male, 40 lbs (18 kg) per parents.
M — Difficulty breathing / asthma exacerbation.
I — Known asthma since age 2; rescue inhaler unavailable at grandmother's house; wheezing and chest tightness began after outdoor play approximately 30 minutes ago on a high-pollen day.
S — SpO2 improved from 94% to 99%. RR improved from 32 to 22. HR 138 — expected tachycardia from albuterol, communicated to parents. Wheeze markedly reduced bilaterally. GCS 15, position of comfort, speaking in full sentences.
T — Albuterol 2.5 mg via small-volume nebulizer at 6 LPM administered approximately 8 minutes ago with improved SpO2, RR, WOB, and wheeze. Ready for transport/turnover.
exemplar_narrative:
Squad 1 was dispatched to 2847 Maple Street for a 4-year-old male with difficulty breathing. Upon arrival, patient Liam, a 4-year-old male weighing approximately 40 lbs, was found seated in a tripod position on the living room couch. Audible expiratory wheeze was noted from the doorway. Mother reported onset approximately 30 minutes prior following outdoor play on a high-pollen day. Known asthma diagnosed at age 2 with two prior ED visits, last 6 months ago. Prescribed albuterol MDI was at grandmother's house and not available. NKDA. No fever per parents.

Primary assessment: SpO2 94%, RR 32 breaths/min with intercostal retractions and nasal flaring, HR 128 bpm, GCS 15. Lung sounds: bilateral expiratory wheeze with decreased air movement at bases. Patient maintained in position of comfort (self-selected tripod). Albuterol 2.5 mg via small-volume nebulizer set up and administered at 6 LPM per protocol — 5-rights cross-check verbalized to partner. Oxygen support was provided per active protocol. Patient tolerated treatment. Parents advised of expected tachycardia as an albuterol side effect.

Post-treatment reassessment: SpO2 improved to 99%, RR decreased to 22 breaths/min, HR 138 bpm, lung sounds cleared bilaterally with improved air movement. Patient speaking in full sentences and more comfortable. Patient care transferred to Medic 1 ALS crew upon arrival with full DMIST report provided.

debrief.condition_background: Liam is a 4-year-old with a known history of asthma who developed acute bronchospasm after outdoor play on a high-pollen day. His albuterol MDI was unavailable, making EMS intervention with a nebulized albuterol treatment the critical treatment. The presentation is moderate respiratory distress — tachypnea (RR 32), SpO2 94%, audible expiratory wheeze, intercostal retractions, and tripod positioning. Untreated, this patient can deteriorate to severe respiratory distress and ultimately respiratory failure. Oxygen support should follow the active NASEMSO respiratory distress rubric and local protocol. Albuterol causes expected tachycardia — this is a known side effect, not a contraindication. A 'silent chest' (loss of wheeze in a wheezing patient) is an ominous late sign indicating critically decreased air movement.
key_teaching_points: Tripod position (leaning forward, hands on knees) is a classic sign of significant respiratory distress — the patient is using accessory muscles
The Pediatric Assessment Triangle (PAT): Appearance, Work of Breathing, Circulation to skin — complete in the first 15 seconds
Albuterol 2.5 mg via SVN (small volume nebulizer) is the definitive BLS treatment for reactive airway disease — administer per protocol
OPQRST: Onset, Provocation/Palliation, Quality, Region/Radiation, Severity, Time — use this for the chief complaint
SAMPLE: Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Pertinent medical history, Last oral intake, Events leading to call
Oxygen support decisions should follow the active respiratory distress rubric and SpO2/work-of-breathing trend
Albuterol causes expected tachycardia — monitor but don't panic if HR increases after treatment
A near-silent chest (loss of wheeze) in a wheezing patient is a late ominous sign — it means critically reduced air movement
Pediatric patients can decompensate RAPIDLY — a child 'tired' of breathing is a child about to crash
common_mistakes: Laying the patient flat — always allow position of comfort for respiratory distress
Delaying needed respiratory support while obtaining extended history
Failing to reassess SpO2, work of breathing, and lung sounds after treatment
Not administering albuterol SVN — this is the definitive BLS treatment — administer per protocol
Not reassessing and documenting vitals after interventions
Incomplete OPQRST or SAMPLE — missing severity, time course, or medication history

checklist.description: PAT completed — appearance (anxious, mild distress), work of breathing (retractions, audible wheeze), and circulation to skin assessed
checklist.done_feedback: PAT completed — anxious appearance, retractions and audible wheeze (WOB), and circulation to skin assessed before hands-on contact.
checklist.missed_feedback: PAT not completed — in respiratory distress the visual PAT drives oxygen delivery strategy and treatment priority before vitals are obtained.
checklist.clinical_rationale: PAT classifies respiratory distress as upper or lower airway and assigns sick/not-sick severity before any intervention; this drives the entire treatment algorithm.
checklist.common_error: Performing vitals before completing the PAT — in moderate-severe respiratory distress, the visual PAT should direct immediate interventions before any cuff or sensor is placed.

checklist.description: Albuterol 2.5 mg via SVN (small volume nebulizer) administered per protocol for pediatric bronchospasm
checklist.done_feedback: Albuterol 2.5 mg via SVN administered — correct first-line bronchodilator; expect SpO₂ improvement and reduced WOB within 5–10 minutes.
checklist.missed_feedback: Albuterol SVN not administered — bronchospasm is the primary reversible cause of wheeze in a known asthmatic; withholding it leaves airway obstruction untreated during transport.
checklist.clinical_rationale: Beta-2 agonist bronchodilation is the protocol-mandated first-line treatment for asthma exacerbation; albuterol relaxes bronchial smooth muscle within 5 minutes.
checklist.common_error: Waiting for SpO₂ to drop below 90% before administering albuterol — treat bronchospasm on clinical signs (wheeze, retractions, tachypnea) and history, not oximetry threshold alone.

checklist.description: Foreign body aspiration screened out — history consistent with asthma exacerbation (known asthmatic, gradual onset, no sudden choke event)
checklist.done_feedback: Foreign body aspiration screened out — known asthmatic with gradual onset after outdoor play; no sudden choke event or history of eating small objects.
checklist.missed_feedback: Foreign body aspiration not screened out — any wheeze in a young child requires differential evaluation; unrecognized foreign body leads to delayed referral and progressive obstruction.
checklist.clinical_rationale: In children under 5, wheeze can result from a foreign body rather than reactive airway disease; history of sudden onset, meal or play with small objects, and asymmetric breath sounds drive the differential.
checklist.common_error: Assuming all wheeze in a child with known asthma is an exacerbation — a foreign body can coexist with asthma; sudden unilateral wheeze or asymmetric breath sounds increase suspicion.

checklist.description: IV/IO access not attempted — outside BLS scope for pediatric asthma management
checklist.done_feedback: IV/IO correctly avoided — albuterol SVN is the BLS-scope treatment for asthma; IV access is an ALS skill not required for this presentation.
checklist.missed_feedback: IV/IO attempted — outside BLS scope; nebulized albuterol is the correct BLS intervention for bronchospasm.
checklist.clinical_rationale: BLS management of asthma exacerbation is supplemental O2 and nebulized albuterol; IV access, magnesium, and epinephrine administration belong to ALS scope.

impression_challenge.prompt: Based on your initial assessment, what is your primary clinical impression?
